text: The surgeon begins by dissecting the angular incisure, the angle between the esophagus and the proximal stomach, using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon dissects the lesser curvature of the stomach and creates an opening through the lesser omentum, gaining entry into the lesser sac during the laparoscopic gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon opens the lesser sac, revealing the space between the stomach and the pancreas during the laparoscopic gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon uses a blue load stapler to construct a 5 to 6 centimeter gastric pouch from the lesser curvature of the stomach.
The surgeon constructs the gastric pouch around an Evo tube, which is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs the final staple firing to complete the construction of the gastric pouch, securing the tissue along the lesser curvature.
The surgeon examines the ligament of Treitz, with the inferior mesenteric vein visible adjacent to it.
The surgeon measures approximately forty centimeters along the small bowel, likely preparing for the next step in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon uses a white load stapler to transect the small bowel, visibly dividing it into two segments.
On the right side of the screen, the small bowel is identified as the biliopancreatic limb, while the left side shows the Roux limb. A clip is placed on the biliopancreatic limb for marking.
The surgeon brings the newly created limb of the small bowel up to the gastric pouch, preparing for anastomosis.
The surgeon constructs a two-layer hand-sewn anastomosis using two micro-stitches, joining the gastric pouch to the small bowel.
The surgeon ties the first stitch of the hand-sewn anastomosis, securing the connection between the gastric pouch and the new limb.
The surgeon runs the first layer of sutures along the extension of the anastomosis, completing the initial layer of the two-layer hand-sewn anastomosis.
The surgeon uses a manic scalpel to create an opening in the gastric pouch, with the incision visibly taking place in the surgical field.
The surgeon measures the opening in the gastric pouch to approximately 1.5 centimeters and proceeds to create an opening in the Roux limb.
The surgeon assesses the size of the opening on the root limb, noting it is slightly smaller than the previous opening due to stretching.
The surgeon constructs the second layer of the anastomosis using two micro stitches, carefully securing the corners of the tissue.
The surgeon takes full-thickness layers of both the small bowel and the gastric pouch, preparing to construct the anastomosis.
The surgeon takes separate bites with sutures at the corners of the anastomosis for increased precision, focusing on the detailed stitching of the gastric pouch and root limb.
The surgeon stops stitching just before reaching the corner of the anastomosis and begins a new stitch on the right side of the anastomosis.
The surgeon places four separate stitches to secure the anastomosis, ensuring precise alignment and closure of the tissue layers.
The surgeon passes the EO-II device from the gastric pouch into the small bowel, facilitating the connection between these two anatomical structures.
The surgeon carefully stitches the anterior wall of the anastomosis, ensuring that the back wall is not inadvertently included in the suturing process.
The surgeon completes the final layer of the anastomosis using gaiter stitches and two micro stitches, ensuring precise closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon prepares to test the anastomosis by introducing air into the gastric pouch and submerging the anastomosis under water to check for leaks.
The surgeon measures and runs the rule-limb for approximately 100 to 150 centimeters to prepare for constructing the gastrojejunostomy.
The surgeon places stay stitches between the Roux limb and the biliopancreatic limb, followed by making enterotomies in each limb.
The surgeon constructs the anastomosis using a 60mm stapler with a white load, visibly aligning the tissue edges for stapling.
The surgeon closes the resulting defect in two layers using 2-0 Vicryl stitches, carefully placing the sutures around the previously constructed anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using two or six sutures, employing a non-exoperable stitch connector. The defect is closed with separate four-stroke sutures.
The completed jugular jejunostomy is visible, showing the connection between the jejunum and another segment of the gastrointestinal tract.
The surgeon uses silk sutures to close a pituitous defect, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.
The surgeon performs an endoscopy to visually inspect the gastric bypass and retests the anastomosis for integrity.
